Vibe Coding + Website Building Learning Guide

Build & Publish Your Own Webpage
Google Sites + Copilot Vibe Coding

You do not need to know how to code. You describe what you want, Copilot writes the HTML, and Google Sites puts it online. By the end of this guide you will have a live web address you can send to anyone.

⏱️ 60 min hands-on
🧰 Google Sites + Microsoft 365 Copilot
🎯 2 pages published live
💻 No coding experience needed

📖 How to use this guide

This guide follows the website-creation session exactly as it was run in class. Work through it in order — Part 1 to Part 3 gets your first page live, and Part 4 shows you how to build something genuinely useful for your own job role. Part 5 collects every problem that came up on the day, so check there first when something looks broken.

The grey time pills (like ⏱ 07:20) point to the matching moment in the class recording, so you can watch the exact click if a step is unclear. Every screenshot is taken from that recording.

The idea

1. Sites gives you the shell

Google Sites hosts the page and gives you a real web address. It is free with any Gmail account.

The idea

2. Copilot writes the code

You describe the tool you want in plain English. Copilot returns finished HTML. This is called vibe coding.

The idea

3. Embed joins them up

You paste Copilot's code into a Google Sites full page embed, hit Publish, and it is live on the internet.

🗂️ What's in this guide

1 Set up the page shell in Google Sites 2 Get Copilot to write the code 3 Embed the code and publish 4 Build a real tool for your job role 5 When something goes wrong 6 The golden rules
🏗️

Part 1 · Set up the page shell

Google Sites — create the empty container that will hold your code
01

Open Google Sites and start a blank site ⏱ 01:05

Google Sites is free with any personal Gmail account — you do not need a company Google account for this.

  • Search Google Sites and open the site creator from the results page.
  • Click Sign in (top-right) and sign in with your personal Gmail account.
  • Choose the Blank template to start a new site.
  • You will land on the editor showing a dark banner reading "Your page title". That is your empty site.
02

Add a "full page embed" page — do not click the + button ⏱ 06:17

This is the step people get wrong. A normal new page will not hold your code properly. You need the full page embed option, and it only appears when you hover.

  • On the right-hand panel, click the Pages tab. You will see your Home page listed.
  • Hover your mouse over the + button at the bottom — do not click it.
  • A small menu fans out. Choose Full page embed (not New page).
⚠️ Watch out: if you click the + button instead of hovering, Google Sites creates a plain new page and you will have to delete it and start this step again.
03

Name the page — no spaces ⏱ 07:20

The page name becomes part of the web address, so Google Sites will reject spaces.

  • In the New full page embed box, type a title made of the topic followed by your name — for example digitalskills-khai.
  • Use a hyphen or run the words together. No spaces. A hyphen is fine; a space will be rejected.
  • Click Done.
04

Check you landed on the right screen ⏱ 07:48

This is your checkpoint. If your screen looks like this, Part 1 is done correctly and the page is waiting for code.

  • You should now see an empty page with a < > icon in the middle.
  • Below it are two links: Add embed and Add from Drive.
  • Leave this tab open. You are coming back to it — but first you need some code to paste.
🤖

Part 2 · Get Copilot to write the code

Microsoft 365 Copilot — describe it in plain English, get finished HTML back
05

Open a new Copilot chat and switch the model ⏱ 09:46

Writing code is a complex task. The default model will produce a weaker result — switching the model is the single biggest quality upgrade you can make, and it takes two seconds.

  • Go to the Microsoft 365 Copilot portal and click New chat.
  • At the top of the chat, click the model dropdown (it usually says Auto).
  • Change it to the reasoning / "think deeper" model — in class this was GPT-5.5 Think Deeper.
💡 Rule of thumb: simple question → leave it on Auto. Anything that involves building something (code, analysis, a plan) → switch to the thinking model first.
06

Write your prompt — start simple ⏱ 10:27

Your first page is deliberately easy: a personal work checklist. The point is to prove the whole pipeline works end to end before you attempt anything ambitious.

  • Ask for an HTML page, then list your own checklist items — three or four is plenty.
  • Use your real tasks. Do not copy the example below; the whole point is that it is yours.
  • Press Submit and let it run.
Starter Prompt
Create a HTML code for my work checklist. My work checklist is: 1) [your first task] 2) [your second task] 3) [your third task] 4) [your fourth task]
07

Wait for the code, and check it is complete ⏱ 13:26

Copilot will produce a long block of code. You do not need to understand it — but you do need to know how to tell that it finished.

  • While you can still see the stop button, Copilot is still working. Let it finish.
  • Good code starts with <!DOCTYPE html> / <html>.
  • Scroll to the bottom of the block: it should end with </html>. That is how you know nothing was cut off.
08

Copy all of the code in one click ⏱ 14:26

Do not try to select the code by dragging — you will miss the top or the bottom of it. There is a button for this.

  • Hover over the code block and look for the small clipboard / copy icon at the top-right of it.
  • Click it. You will see the confirmation "All code copied to clipboard".
  • That is your whole page, copied. Now go back to your Google Sites tab.
🚀

Part 3 · Embed the code and publish

Paste, insert, publish — and you are live on the internet
09

Paste into "Embed code" — not "By URL" ⏱ 15:02

The embed dialog opens on the wrong tab by default. Switching tabs is the step everyone forgets.

  • Back in Google Sites, on your embed page, click Add embed.
  • The Embed from the web box opens on the By URL tab. Click across to Embed code.
  • Paste your code into the box (Ctrl+V, or Cmd+V on Mac).
  • Click Next.
10

Preview it, then Insert ⏱ 16:02

Google Sites shows you a live preview before committing. This is your first sight of the thing you just built.

  • A preview of your page appears inside the dialog. Ignore the size of it for now.
  • Click Insert.
  • Your checklist is now sitting inside the Google Sites page.
11

Publish it to the web ⏱ 16:43

Nothing you have done so far is visible to anyone else. Publishing is what puts it online and gives you the link.

  • Click the Publish button in the top-right corner.
  • Give your site a web address — again, no spaces (e.g. digitalskills-yourname). If it is already taken, add your name or a hyphen until it is accepted.
  • Under Search settings, tick "Request public search engines to not display my site" if you do not want this practice page turning up in Google search results.
  • Click Publish.
12

View your live website ⏱ 18:12

This is the moment it becomes real: a working web address you can send to anybody.

  • Click the dropdown arrow next to the Publish button.
  • Choose View published site.
  • Your checklist opens as a real, live, interactive webpage. Tick a box — it works.
🎉 That is the entire pipeline. Everything from here on is the same four moves — prompt → copy code → embed → publish — just with more ambitious pages.
⚙️

Part 4 · Build a real tool for your job role

Use the prompt library, then iterate until it does what you actually need
13

Open the prompt library and find your job role ⏱ 21:36

The checklist proved the method works. Now build something you would genuinely use at work — the prompt library has a ready-made build prompt for every role in the room.

  • Go back to your learner's guide and open the last topic — Topic 6: Prompt Library.
  • Use the Table of Contents to jump to your job role (Business Development, Accounts & Finance, Audit, Corporate Secretarial, Management, Administration, and so on).
  • Each role has several cards. Each card describes a system you could build.
  • Read the description first and understand what it will produce before you build it.
14

Copy the build prompt into a fresh Copilot chat ⏱ 23:23

The build prompts are long and self-contained on purpose — they already contain everything Copilot needs. You do not have to write them yourself.

  • On the card you want, click Copy prompt.
  • Go to Copilot and start a New chat — a clean one, not the checklist conversation.
  • Remember to switch the model to the thinking model again. New chat means the setting resets.
  • Paste the prompt and Submit.
15

Iterate — version 1 is never the finished thing ⏱ 24:36

This is the most important habit in the whole session. Whatever Copilot gives you first is version 1.0. You look at it, find what is missing, and ask for the next version.

Here is exactly how the Sales Pipeline Kanban Board evolved in class — same conversation, three rounds:

  • Version 1 — Copilot builds the board, but fills it with fake sample deals. Useful to look at, useless to actually work with.
  • Version 2 — "I don't want this fake data. Give me a button so I can upload a CSV file and it turns into the board." Now it takes your real data.
  • Version 3 — add a Clear Board button, a downloadable CSV template so you know what columns to use, and an Export so you can get your data back out.
  • Keep going. Each round, ask yourself: what is missing? Then say so, in plain English, in the same chat.
💡 The mindset: you are not "asking an AI for an answer" — you are directing it through revisions, the way you would brief a designer. The limit is your imagination, not your coding ability.
16

Publish it as a second page on the same site ⏱ 27:15

You already know how to do this — it is Parts 1 and 3 again. One site can hold as many embedded tools as you like.

  • Copy the code from Copilot (the clipboard icon again).
  • In Google Sites: Pages → hover the + → Full page embed → give it a name (e.g. kanban board pipeline) → Done.
  • Add embed → Embed code tab → paste → Next → Insert.
  • Publish. Then view the live page.
🔧

Part 5 · When something goes wrong

Every problem that actually came up in the class, and how to fix it
17

"Copilot gave me a file, not a code block" ⏱ 28:44

Sometimes Copilot hands you a downloadable .html file instead of code on screen — and there is no copy button to click. The code is still in there; you just have to open the file to get at it.

  • Click the file, then click Download to get the real file onto your laptop.
  • Find it in your Downloads folder.
  • Right-click the file → Open with → Notepad (every Windows laptop has it). Do not double-click it — that just opens it in a browser.
  • In Notepad: Ctrl+A to select all, then Ctrl+C to copy.
  • That is your code. Go and paste it into the Embed code box as normal.
18

"Some content didn't load — a script didn't run correctly" ⏱ 29:32

Alarming, but harmless. Your page is fine — you are just looking at it in the wrong place.

  • This red warning appears when you preview the HTML file inside SharePoint / the browser preview.
  • That preview is not the actual file — it deliberately blocks the code from running.
  • Ignore it. Download the file and open it properly, or paste the code into Google Sites, and it will work perfectly.
19

"I made a new version — how do I update the page?" ⏱ 53:09

You do not create a new page each time you iterate. You replace the code inside the existing embed.

  • Copy the new version of the code from Copilot.
  • Go back into Google Sites edit mode (not the live published page) and open the page.
  • Click on the embed, then click the Edit (pencil) icon. You will see your existing code.
  • Select all of the old code and delete it, then paste the new code in its place.
  • Next → Save → Publish.
20

"My site opens on a blank page" ⏱ 51:19

Google Sites gives every new site an empty Home page. If you never touched it, that empty page is what visitors land on first — which makes your site look broken.

  • In the Pages panel, hover the page you actually want people to land on.
  • Click its three-dot menu → Set as homepage.
  • Now hover the old, empty Home page → three-dot menu → Delete.
  • Publish — otherwise nothing changes on the live site.

🏆 The golden rules

If you remember nothing else from this session, remember these six.

  • Publish means save. Any change you make in Google Sites — new code, deleted page, new homepage — is invisible to the world until you hit Publish. Every single time.
  • Hover the +, never click it. Full page embed only appears on hover.
  • Switch the model before you build. Coding is a complex task; the thinking model is a different class of result.
  • No spaces in page names or web addresses. Hyphens are fine.
  • Embed code, not By URL. The dialog opens on the wrong tab.
  • Version 1 is a draft. Look at what you got, decide what is missing, and ask for version 2. Then 3. Then 4.

🎈 What "vibe coding" actually means

You have just done it. Vibe coding is describing what you want in ordinary language and letting AI write the code — then refining it by conversation rather than by programming. You can build custom systems, calculators, trackers, dashboards, checklists: whatever your job actually needs.

The only thing limiting you now is your imagination.
